target.title: Test/run state machine clamp guard
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `activeRuns` clamp guard in `onRunEnd()` (`Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)`) was completely untested. Calling `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()` (e.g. error handling paths, race conditions) would cause `activeRuns` to go negative without this guard.
- Why it matters: A negative `activeRuns` would cause the machine to report incorrect state permanently, potentially breaking busy indicators across all channels.
- What changed: Added two unit tests covering the clamp guard — one for calling `onRunEnd()` with no prior `onRunStart()`, and one for calling it more times than `onRunStart()`.
- What did NOT change: No production code was changed. This is a test-only PR covering an existing defensive guard.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: The `Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)` guard in `onRunEnd()` was added defensively but never had test coverage to verify it works correctly.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No unit test for mismatched `onRunStart`/`onRunEnd` calls.
- Contributing context (if known): Error handling paths or race conditions can trigger `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/channels/run-state-machine.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `activeRuns` never goes below zero regardless of call order.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, directly tests the guard condition.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — two tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
None.

## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[onRunEnd x2] -> activeRuns = -1 (without guard, would break busy state)

After:
[onRunEnd x2] -> activeRuns = 0 (guard clamps correctly, now verified by test)
```

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A

### Steps
1. Call `machine.onRunEnd()` without calling `onRunStart()`
2. Check `activeRuns` value

### Expected
- `activeRuns` stays at 0

### Actual (before test)
- Guard existed but was unverified by any test

## Evidence
- [x] New unit tests added covering both scenarios

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: `onRunEnd` with no prior `onRunStart`, `onRunEnd` called twice after one `onRunStart`
- Edge cases checked: `activeRuns` stays at 0 in both cases
- What you did NOT verify: Race conditions in a live runtime

None.

target.title: feat: expose model pricing to plugins via runtime.usage API
target.body:
## Summary
Adds a `runtime.usage` namespace to the plugin runtime, exposing `resolveModelCostConfig()` and `estimateUsageCost()` so context engine plugins can calculate model-cost estimates from OpenClaw's configured pricing instead of hardcoded heuristics.

## Motivation
Context engine plugins, such as [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), truncate stale or large context to save tokens. Today they can only estimate savings with a fixed heuristic. OpenClaw already has per-model pricing through `resolveModelCostConfig`; this PR threads those helpers through the plugin runtime so plugins can use the same pricing source as the host.

## Changes
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts` — adds the runtime usage facade.
- `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` — adds the `usage` namespace to `PluginRuntimeCore`.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` — wires `createRuntimeUsage()` into runtime assembly.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` — updates the plugin runtime mock.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` — asserts the runtime exposes both usage helpers.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md` — documents the plugin SDK usage helpers.
- `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` — regenerates the public SDK API baseline.

## Real Behavior Proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin can access the new `api.runtime.usage` namespace and call both pricing helpers without importing OpenClaw internals.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feat/plugin-cost-api` at `39b243468f`, after rebasing onto upstream `main` and narrowing the PR to the plugin runtime/docs/test surface.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx .artifacts/pr64436-runtime-usage-proof/run-direct-plugin-api-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from a minimal external plugin fixture with `openclaw.plugin.json` plus `index.mjs`; its `register(api)` calls `api.runtime.usage.resolveModelCostConfig(...)` and `api.runtime.usage.estimateUsageCost(...)`.

```text
[proof] repoHead=39b243468f
[proof] pluginDir=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im
[proof] manifest=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im/openclaw.plugin.json
[plugin] id=runtime-usage-proof
[plugin] hasRuntimeUsage=true
[plugin] resolveModelCostConfig cost={"input":3,"output":15,"cacheRead":0.3,"cacheWrite":3.75}
[plugin] estimateUsageCost total=0.010950
[proof] PASS plugin register(api) called both api.runtime.usage helpers
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The plugin saw `api.runtime.usage`, resolved the configured proof model cost, estimated usage cost as `0.010950`, and exited with the PASS line above.

- **What was not tested:** Full OpenClaw app startup and provider billing reconciliation; this proof is scoped to the plugin runtime API surface added by this PR.

## Local Verification
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `pnpm format:check docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.ts src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts -t 'exposes runtime.usage pricing helpers' --reporter=dot`

## Related
- Issue #64085 — Provider circuit breaker cost/status reporting.
- PR #64127 — companion status-provider work.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

target.title: fix(gateway): use already-aborted signal in stopChannel fallback
target.body:
## Summary

- In `stopChannel`, when no `AbortController` exists for an account (i.e. no running task), the fallback `new AbortController().signal` creates a signal from an anonymous controller that **can never be aborted**
- `stopAccount` implementations may use this signal for cleanup coordination (checking `signal.aborted`, passing to `fetch`, using with `sleepWithAbort`, etc.)
- A never-aborting signal can cause `stopAccount` to hang or timeout during graceful shutdown instead of completing immediately

### Fix

Replace `new AbortController().signal` with `AbortSignal.abort()`, which returns an already-aborted signal. This correctly communicates "this account is being stopped" to the plugin's `stopAccount` implementation.

### Context

On line 485, `abort?.abort()` is called — so if `abort` exists, its signal is already aborted. The fallback only fires when `abort` is undefined (no active task for this account). In both cases the intent is the same: tell `stopAccount` to shut down. `AbortSignal.abort()` makes the fallback consistent with the primary path.

## Test plan

- [x] All 14 tests in `server-channels.test.ts` pass
- [x] One-line change, minimal risk
- [x] `AbortSignal.abort()` is available since Node 15.12.0; project requires Node 22+

[AI-assisted]

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

### Actual

target.title: doctor(memory): surface FTS5 unavailable state in doctor checks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #62328 by making `openclaw doctor` visibly report when SQLite FTS5 is unavailable in the running gateway.

- Extend `doctor.memory.status` payload with `fts` probe fields (`enabled`, `available`, `error`)
- Thread that payload through `probeGatewayMemoryStatus` in doctor command flow
- Add a dedicated doctor warning in `noteMemorySearchHealth` when gateway reports FTS unavailable
- Include targeted guidance for `no such module: fts5` / missing `ENABLE_FTS5`

## Why

Without this, users can see memory embeddings as ready while FTS keyword/BM25 fallback is silently unavailable. This change surfaces the condition and gives concrete next steps.

## Tests

- Added/updated test in `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` to assert `fts` is returned in `doctor.memory.status`
- Added test in `src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts` to assert doctor emits an FTS5 warning when gateway probe reports unavailable
- Local targeted verification:
  - `corepack pnpm exec vitest src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts -t "returns gateway embedding probe status for the default agent"`
  - `corepack pnpm exec vitest src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts -t "warns when gateway probe reports FTS unavailable"`

## Notes

One unrelated existing test in `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` is currently path-sensitive on this Windows environment (`falls back to the manager workspace...`) and fails independently of this change. The new/affected targeted cases pass.

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.

target.title: feat(plugins): upgrade llm_input/llm_output hooks from fire-and-forget to modifying
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `llm_input` and `llm_output` plugin hooks are fire-and-forget (observe-only). Plugins cannot block, modify, or redact LLM calls/responses.
- Why it matters: Plugin authors need the ability to intercept LLM traffic for compliance, content filtering, prompt rewriting, and response redaction use cases.
- What changed: Both hooks now run sequentially via `runModifyingHook`. `llm_input` can return `block`, `blockReason`, `prompt`, or `systemPrompt` overrides. `llm_output` can return replacement `assistantTexts`. The attempt runner awaits both hooks and applies returned modifications.
- Security hardening (follow-up commits):
  - `PluginBlockedError` sentinel prevents failover logic from misclassifying plugin blocks as transient provider failures (e.g. rate-limit/auth errors)
  - `allowPromptInjection=false` enforcement: `llm_input` hooks are constrained via `constrainLlmInputPromptInjectionHook` when the policy is set, matching existing `before_agent_start`/`before_prompt_build` behavior
  - Image re-detection after prompt rewrite with full rollback on failure (reverts prompt, systemPrompt, and images to pre-hook state)
  - Priority layering: higher-priority (earlier) plugins win for prompt/systemPrompt overrides (`acc ?? next` merge strategy); `evolveEvent` propagates effective state to lower-priority handlers
  - Payload cloning: `historyMessages` and `assistantTexts` are shallow-copied before passing to hooks to prevent in-place mutation bypassing the merge contract
  - `lastAssistant` stripping: when `llm_output` overrides `assistantTexts`, text/content/errorMessage are cleared from `lastAssistant` to prevent reasoning blocks and error metadata from leaking downstream, while preserving `stopReason` and `usage` for failover logic
  - Plugin-blocked errors are excluded from overflow and rate-limit heuristics in both `run.ts` and `agent-runner-execution.ts`
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook event shapes are unchanged (additive result types only). Plugins that return `void` behave identically to before. No changes to other hooks, plugin discovery, or the Plugin SDK public surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #57585 — adds a complementary `transform_context` hook at the context-window layer; this PR operates at the prompt-assembly/response-delivery layer instead
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can now return `{ block: true, blockReason: "..." }` to prevent an LLM call from being made.
- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can return `{ prompt, systemPrompt }` to override the prompt/system prompt sent to the LLM.
- Plugins registering `llm_output` hooks can return `{ assistantTexts: [...] }` to replace the assistant response (e.g. for redaction).
- Existing plugins that return `void` from these hooks are unaffected — fully backward compatible.
- When `allowPromptInjection=false` is set for a plugin, `llm_input` prompt/systemPrompt fields are stripped from the hook result (block/blockReason still honored).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[prompt ready] -> llm_input (fire-and-forget, no return) -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> llm_output (fire-and-forget) -> [delivered]

After:
[prompt ready] -> await llm_input -> [block? -> PluginBlockedError] or [apply prompt/systemPrompt overrides -> re-detect images] -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> await llm_output -> [apply assistantTexts override, strip lastAssistant] -> [delivered]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Plugins can now modify prompts and block/alter LLM responses. This is gated behind the existing plugin hook registration mechanism — only installed and enabled plugins can register these hooks. The blocking path throws a dedicated `PluginBlockedError` sentinel that is logged and excluded from failover/overflow misclassification. Prompt modifications are subject to `allowPromptInjection` policy enforcement. Hook payloads are cloned to prevent mutation. Response overrides strip original content from `lastAssistant` to prevent data leaks. Prompt/response modifications flow through the same trust boundary as existing modifying hooks (e.g. `before_tool_call`).

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Plugin with `llm_input`/`llm_output` hook registered

### Steps

1. Register a plugin with an `llm_input` hook that returns `{ block: true, blockReason: "test" }`
2. Trigger an agent run
3. Observe the LLM call is blocked with the logged reason

### Expected

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

### Actual

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

## Evidence

- [x] Code review hardening: all 12 review comments from automated reviewers (Codex/Greptile) addressed across 9 follow-up commits
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: manual gateway test with a local test plugin confirmed `llm_input` and `llm_output` hooks fire correctly on v2026.4.1 gateway. Logs show `llm_input fired` with prompt rewrite (`-> rewriting prompt (prepending tag)`), `llm_output fired` with `assistantTexts (1)` and usage data, and `-> BLOCKING: prompt contains "BLOCKED"` for the block path. The released gateway treats hooks as fire-and-forget (return values ignored), confirming backward compatibility; modifying behavior (block/rewrite/redact) is validated by 22 automated tests.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Hook blocking, prompt override, response redaction via test plugins, multi-plugin priority chaining with `evolveEvent` propagation
- Manual gateway testing: installed a local test plugin (`llm-hook-test`) on a live v2026.4.1 gateway via `openclaw plugins install -l`. Confirmed hook registration, `llm_input` firing with prompt data (provider, model, sessionId, imagesCount), `llm_output` firing with assistantTexts and usage. Tested block, rewrite, redact, and observe scenarios through the dashboard web UI. Verified the released gateway's fire-and-forget path is unaffected (backward compat).
- Automated tests: 22/22 tests pass in `wired-hooks-llm-modifying.test.ts` covering block result, prompt/systemPrompt override, evolveEvent propagation, multi-plugin priority merge, backward compat (void returns), and error handling (graceful degradation).
- Edge cases checked: Plugin returning void (backward compat), hook failure graceful degradation (logged warning, continues), `allowPromptInjection=false` constraint, image re-detection failure rollback, empty prompt override (`prompt: ""`), `PluginBlockedError` bypassing failover/overflow heuristics
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end production load with many concurrent plugins across multiple providers

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A misbehaving plugin could block all LLM calls or silently rewrite prompts.
  - Mitigation: Blocking is logged with reason via dedicated `PluginBlockedError`. Prompt/response modifications are opt-in (plugin must explicitly return override fields). `allowPromptInjection=false` can constrain prompt modification. Hook payloads are cloned. Existing hook failure handling catches and logs errors gracefully without crashing the run. Higher-priority plugins take precedence via first-setter-wins merge strategy.

target.title: Docker: add Mac migration and keep-awake helpers
target.body:
## What
Add operator helpers for Mac-hosted Docker deployments.

## Why
Running OpenClaw in Docker on macOS is easier to operate if the repo includes a simple host-awake helper and a low-disruption backup/restore path for moving from an Intel Mac to Apple Silicon.

## Included
- add `scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh` to keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions
- add `scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh` and `scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh` for migration backups
- preserve config, workspace, auth-profile secret-key state, repo `.env`, and Docker setup files
- harden backup archive and checksum file permissions and verify the external archive checksum before extraction
- add a focused migration-helper regression test and Docker docs

## Notes
This PR is intentionally separate from the Docker runtime/image PR so runtime behavior and host-operator helpers can be reviewed independently.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mac-hosted Docker operator helpers can preserve OpenClaw config, workspace scripts, and auth-profile secret-key state during host migration, and can keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions.
- **Real environment tested**: Intel macOS host with Docker Desktop and the existing OpenClaw Docker deployment; plus a local restore fixture that exercises the backup/restore scripts without touching the live config.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `bash -n scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/lib.sh scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh`; `pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`; `pnpm docs:list`; `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`; `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD`.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
bash syntax ok

pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
✓ openclaw Docker migration helpers > backs up and restores config, workspace, auth-profile secrets, and archive permissions
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard

docs:list ok (1313 lines)
check-no-conflict-markers passed
git diff --check passed
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The migration test creates a real archive, verifies the companion `.sha256`, proves the archive/checksum are owner-only, restores config/workspace/auth-profile secret files, and verifies restored `.env` permissions. The earlier live Docker proof also confirmed the backup preserves the digest and portfolio workspace scripts used by the deployed gateway.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a destructive restore over the active live config on the same machine; the restore proof uses an isolated temp fixture so the live Docker deployment is not disturbed.

target.title: fix(usage): handle Daylight saving time (DST) hour boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

This fixes a Control UI Usage-page freeze when a long session spans a local Daylight saving time (DST) transition.

Today the hour-slicing code computes a local `:59:59.999` hour end from the current wall-clock time. In a repeated local hour at Daylight saving time (DST) fallback, that can resolve to an earlier instant and stop cursor progress.

This change switches Usage hour slicing and selected-hour filtering to monotonic next-hour-boundary stepping so aggregation always moves forward in the selected timezone.

## Why

This fixes cases where:

- Usage is rendered in local time
- a session spans a Daylight saving time (DST) fallback or spring-forward boundary
- the page builds hour buckets or applies a selected-hour filter

Without this change, the Control UI can wedge the browser main thread while rendering Usage data for long sessions.

## Changes

- replace the ambiguous local end-of-hour calculation with `getNextHourStart()` plus bounded next-hour stepping in `ui/src/ui/views/usage-metrics.ts`
- convert session hour slicing to half-open intervals `[cursor, nextBoundary)` so exact boundaries do not create zero-duration buckets
- route the selected-hour filter in `ui/src/ui/views/usage.ts` through the same DST-safe helper instead of maintaining a duplicate loop
- add focused node-side regression coverage for Daylight saving time (DST) fallback, Daylight saving time (DST) spring-forward, peak-error aggregation, and full Usage render with an hour filter

## Testing

AI-assisted: yes
Built and tested in: Codex App
Model: GPT-5.4
Reasoning effort: Extra High
Testing: focused local test

- `pnpm --dir ui exec vitest run --config vitest.node.config.ts src/ui/views/usage-metrics.node.test.ts`

- the Claw slice currently covers mission creation, control, state persistence, scopes, and UI scaffolding; the full autonomous execution engine is still a follow-up layer

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: feat(agents): derive fallbacks from configured model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw only derives failover candidates from `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`, so operators who keep multiple models in `agents.defaults.models` still have to duplicate that ordered list manually.
- Why it matters: if the configured primary becomes unavailable, the runtime stops at the primary unless the same catalog is copied into `fallbacks`, which is easy to miss and easy to drift.
- What changed: added an explicit opt-in `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true` that derives fallback candidates from `agents.defaults.models` in config order when explicit `fallbacks` are omitted.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change existing fallback error classification, auth-profile rotation behavior, or the meaning of explicit `fallbacks` / `fallbacks: []`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #60919
- Related #47434
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the fallback resolver only consulted explicit fallback arrays (`fallbacksOverride` or `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`). `agents.defaults.models` remained allowlist/catalog-only, so keeping multiple configured models there did not influence failover.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no opt-in config path that said “use my configured model catalog as the fallback pool when I omit explicit fallbacks.”
- Contributing context (if known): operators often keep a single ordered model catalog in `agents.defaults.models`, so the duplication requirement was easy to miss.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: derive failover candidates from `agents.defaults.models` only when `fallbacksFromModels: true`, keep explicit `fallbacks` precedence, and keep `fallbacks: []` as an intentional disable.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to fallback candidate construction before provider execution.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing `runWithModelFallback` unit coverage already covers explicit fallback ordering and allowlist behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New opt-in config: `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true`
- When enabled and explicit `fallbacks` are omitted, OpenClaw derives fallback candidates from `agents.defaults.models` in config order, excluding the configured primary.
- Explicit `fallbacks` still win, and `fallbacks: []` still disables fallback.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[primary model fails] -> [check explicit fallbacks only] -> [stop if none configured]

After:
[primary model fails] -> [explicit fallbacks?]
                    -> [if none + fallbacksFromModels=true, derive from models catalog]
                    -> [try next configured model]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: fallback candidate construction only (no provider-specific network dependency)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.primary`, `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels`, `agents.defaults.models`

### Steps

1. Configure a primary model plus multiple entries in `agents.defaults.models`.
2. Enable `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true` without setting explicit `fallbacks`.
3. Trigger a failover-worthy primary error.

### Expected

- The next configured model from `agents.defaults.models` is attempted.

### Actual

- Before this PR, the run stopped at the primary unless the fallback list was duplicated manually.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - new unit coverage for catalog-derived fallback
  - explicit `fallbacks` still override catalog derivation
  - explicit `fallbacks: []` still disables fallback
  - config schema + baseline regeneration
- Edge cases checked:
  - configured primary is excluded from derived catalog fallbacks
  - duplicate catalog keys are deduped before candidate construction
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository `build` / `check` green, because latest `main` is already red on unrelated `skills/SourceInfo` and `provider-transport-fetch` issues
  - full `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` run without unrelated unhandled plugin-registration/import noise on current `main`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (Yes)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: operators may assume the models catalog now always implies fallback.
  - Mitigation: the behavior is explicit opt-in (`fallbacksFromModels: true`) and docs clarify that explicit `fallbacks` still take precedence.
- Risk: derived catalog order may surprise users if they keep a broad manual allowlist.
  - Mitigation: opt-in only, preserves config order, and `fallbacks: []` remains the escape hatch to disable fallback.

## Validation notes

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts -t 'derives fallbacks from configured models when enabled|prefers explicit fallbacks over configured model derivation|treats explicit empty fallbacks as disabling configured model derivation'`
  - 3 targeted tests passed
  - current `main` still emits unrelated unhandled plugin registration/import errors during this file
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts`
  - passed (`2` files / `6` tests)
- `pnpm build`
  - fails on existing upstream errors in `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`, `src/agents/skills/local-loader.ts`, `src/agents/skills/skill-contract.ts`
- `pnpm check`
  - fails on the same existing upstream `skills/SourceInfo` errors

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
```
