target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(discord): record history entry when dropping bot message in allowBots=mentions mode
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `allowBots=mentions` is configured on a Discord account, bot messages that are dropped (no mention) skip `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()`, causing gaps in guild channel history. Other bots in the same channel lose context about dropped messages.
- **Why it matters:** In multi-bot setups, history continuity is critical for context-aware responses. Missing history entries cause bots to "forget" recent bot messages in the channel.
- **What changed:** Added `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` call in the `allowBots=mentions` drop path before `return null`, matching the pattern already used in the `requireMention` drop path.
- **What did NOT change:** No logic change to when messages are dropped; only ensures history is recorded before dropping.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `allowBots=mentions` drop path was added without the `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` call that other drop paths already have.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for history recording in the `allowBots=mentions` drop path.
- Prior context: The `requireMention` drop path at line ~900 already records history before returning null.
- Why this regressed now: The `allowBots` feature was added after the history recording pattern was established.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: When a bot message is dropped due to missing mention in `allowBots=mentions` mode, the guild history map should still contain the dropped message entry.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit test directly verifies the history map state after the drop.
- Existing test that already covers this: None previously; added in this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

In multi-bot Discord setups with `allowBots=mentions`, bot messages that are not directly mentioned are now properly recorded in channel history, improving context continuity for other bots.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[bot message, no @mention] -> drop (history NOT recorded) -> other bots lose context

After:
[bot message, no @mention] -> record history -> drop -> other bots retain context
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node.js 22
- Integration/channel: Discord (multi-bot gateway, 6 bots)

### Steps

1. Configure 2+ Discord bot accounts with `allowBots: "mentions"`
2. Bot A sends a message in a shared channel without @mentioning Bot B
3. Bot B drops the message (correct behavior)
4. Check Bot B's guild history for the channel

### Expected

Bot B's guild history contains Bot A's dropped message.

### Actual

Before fix: history entry missing. After fix: history entry recorded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- Test: `pnpm test -- extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts` — "records history when dropping bot message without mention (allowBots=mentions)" passes.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Bot message drop with history recording, bot message with mention (still passes through)
- Edge cases checked: PluralKit bots (exempt from allowBots gate), DM messages (no history recording needed)
- What you did **not** verify: History playback in actual LLM responses across bots

target.title: Add prototype pollution guard to resolveConfigPath
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveConfigPath` in `src/shared/config-eval.ts` traverses config objects by dot-separated path strings (e.g. `"foo.bar.baz"`), but does not check whether any path segment is a dangerous prototype key (`__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor`). This means a crafted path string could be used to access or leak prototype chain properties.

Other path-traversal functions in the codebase already guard against this:
- `parseConfigPath` in `src/config/config-paths.ts`
- `merge-patch.ts`, `runtime-overrides.ts`, `includes.ts`, and others

All of these use the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` utility from `src/infra/prototype-keys.ts`.

## Changes

- Import `isBlockedObjectKey` from `../infra/prototype-keys.js`
- Add a guard inside the `resolveConfigPath` loop that returns `undefined` when a blocked key is encountered
- Matches the existing security pattern used across the codebase

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing tests for `resolveConfigPath` and `isConfigPathTruthyWithDefaults` continue to pass
- [ ] Paths containing `__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor` segments now return `undefined`
- [ ] Normal dot-separated paths still resolve correctly

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `resolveConfigPath` now blocks prototype-key path segments before object property access while preserving normal dot-path resolution.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59694`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
rg -n "isBlockedObjectKey|blocks prototype keys" src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/shared/config-eval.test.ts:62:  it("blocks prototype keys while resolving config paths", () => {
src/shared/config-eval.ts:4:import { isBlockedObjectKey } from "../infra/prototype-keys.js";
src/shared/config-eval.ts:31:    if (isBlockedObjectKey(part)) {
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched resolver has a real guard before object access and the regression coverage keeps `safe.enabled` resolving while `__proto__`, `constructor.name`, and `prototype.polluted` return `undefined`.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `b11430d` (after the OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via `shortenHomePath`, which only redacts the *effective* OpenClaw home. When `OPENCLAW_HOME` points to a directory other than the OS home, a Node binary under the OS home was left unredacted, leaking the OS username into copied Doctor output. The latest commit adds a second-pass OS-home redaction (POSIX + Windows) and keeps the contribution detect-only.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `b11430d`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head, OS-home redaction shown):** The first block is the real render on this machine. The second block is the redaction scenario this commit fixes: with `OPENCLAW_HOME` set elsewhere and Node living under the user's OS home, the exec path is collapsed to `~/...` so the OS username is not leaked. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios computed against the real current date.
```
=== Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
OPENCLAW_HOME      : /srv/openclaw
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 25 (25.6.1, in maintenance) ===
Node 25 is in maintenance mode (EOL 2026-06-01, ~1 month remaining).
Consider upgrading to a current Active LTS release for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~11 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). In the redaction scenario, with `OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw` and Node under the user's OS home, the rendered exec path is `~/.nvm/...` — the OS username does not appear. Before this commit, `shortenHomePath` alone returned the path unchanged (it only redacts the OpenClaw home `/srv/openclaw`, not the OS home), so the username leaked. Injected Node 25 / 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX and Windows `OPENCLAW_HOME`-override regression tests cover the redaction case; 50 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: utils: add AbortSignal support to runTasksWithConcurrency
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `runTasksWithConcurrency` lacks external cancellation support, making it impossible for callers to abort pending tasks when an operation is no longer needed.
- Why it matters: The codebase already uses `AbortSignal` in `fetch-timeout.ts` and other async utilities; the concurrency helper should follow the same pattern.
- What changed: Added an optional `signal?: AbortSignal` parameter. Workers check `signal.aborted` before picking up new tasks. Already-running tasks complete normally.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to existing parameters, return type, or behavior when `signal` is not provided. Fully backward compatible.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- N/A (standalone improvement)

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/utils/run-with-concurrency.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: abort mid-execution stops new task pickup; pre-aborted signal skips all tasks
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Unit tests directly exercise the signal check in the worker loop

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None (new optional parameter only)

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 22.04
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2

### Steps

1. Import `runTasksWithConcurrency`
2. Pass an `AbortController.signal` as the `signal` parameter
3. Call `controller.abort()` during task execution

### Expected

- Workers stop picking up new tasks after abort

### Actual

- Confirmed: remaining tasks are skipped, completed tasks retain their results

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after: 5/5 tests pass including 2 new signal tests

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: abort mid-run, pre-aborted signal, existing behavior unchanged

None

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: fix(acp): persist spawn labels in target session store
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` persisted the spawned ACP session metadata into the target agent store, but tried to persist the label into the requester store.
- Why it matters: Cross-agent ACP spawns (for example from a Telegram topic into a `codex` ACP session) silently lost the requested label, so follow-up commands like `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` could not resolve the spawned session by label.
- What changed: `persistSpawnedSessionLabel(...)` now resolves the target session store from the spawned ACP `sessionKey` before writing the label, and only mutates the in-memory requester store when both stores are actually the same.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn routing, binding behavior, and runtime option handling are unchanged.
- AI-assisted: Yes. This PR was prepared with Codex.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP sessions spawned with `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` now retain the requested label even when the spawned session is written to a different agent session store than the requester. Label-based follow-up commands such as `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` now work in those cross-agent ACP flows.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
- Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source install + local dev checkout
- Model/provider: ACPX (`codex` / `gemini` harnesses)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram ACP topic binding
- Relevant config (redacted): `acp.enabled=true`, `acp.backend=acpx`, `acp.allowedAgents=["codex","gemini"]`

### Steps

1. From a Telegram topic with ACP commands enabled, run `/acp spawn codex --bind here --label codex-a2ui --cwd /path/to/worktree`.
2. Observe that the spawned ACP session is created in the `codex` session store.
3. Try to resolve the session by label, for example with `/focus codex-a2ui` or `/acp status codex-a2ui`.

### Expected

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store includes `label: "codex-a2ui"`.
- Label-based session resolution works for follow-up commands.

### Actual (before fix)

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store had `label: null`.
- The label was written against the requester store instead, so label-based resolution failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: local reproduction showed the spawned ACP session persisted in the target store with `label: null` before the fix; after patching the persistence path, the same session entry carried the requested label.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: traced the spawn path, confirmed the label write used the requester `storePath`, reproduced the bug locally with a Telegram-bound ACP `codex` session, then verified the patched code writes to the target session store resolved from the spawned `sessionKey`.
- Edge cases checked: confirmed the in-memory requester store is only mutated when it matches the resolved target store, so same-store spawns still behave normally.
- What you did **not** verify: kept verification minimal per request. This checkout's current `origin/main` also has unrelated missing-dependency/typecheck failures (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `matrix-js-sdk`, and related surfaces), so `pnpm build` and the focused `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts` run did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
- Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
- Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: labels still missing on cross-agent ACP spawns, or label writes regressing for same-store spawns

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The fix touches session-store selection during spawn label persistence, so a bad store-path resolution could break label writes for a subset of ACP spawn flows.
  - Mitigation: Added a regression test that specifically covers a requester-store/target-store mismatch and asserts the label is written to the spawned session store, not the requester store.

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator

target.title: fix(hooks): restore --exclude-labels in Gmail watcher args
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56635

- Restores `--exclude-labels SPAM,TRASH,DRAFT,SENT` in `gog gmail watch serve` args so sent mail and draft saves do not trigger Gmail hook runs by default.
- Adds `hooks.gmail.excludeLabels` to the config schema/types with runtime default resolution and hook override support.
- Updates the sanitized log-args expectation so `--exclude-labels` appears in non-secret watcher logs.
- Regenerates the base config schema and docs baseline hash, and documents the default excluded label set.

target.title: fix: bind Claude permission replies to session
target.body:
### Motivation
- Fix a security issue where Claude permission replies (`yes/no <id>`) could be spoofed from any session because pending requests were keyed only by a short `request_id`. 
- Ensure permission replies are only accepted from the same session that originated the permission request to prevent cross-session or untrusted-channel approvals. 
- Preserve existing behavior for legitimate requests while closing the approval spoofing vector. 

### Description
- Require `session_key` in the `notifications/claude/channel/permission_request` schema and propagate it through the MCP server; changed `src/mcp/channel-shared.ts` and `src/mcp/channel-server.ts`. 
- Store pending Claude permission requests with the originating `sessionKey` and only accept `yes/no <request_id>` replies when the inbound message's `sessionKey` matches; changed `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts`. 
- Add and update tests to cover session-bound permission handling and regression scenarios, including rejecting attacker-session replies and accepting trusted-session replies; changed `src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`. 

### Testing
- Ran the targeted unit tests with `pnpm test -- src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`, which passed (`1 passed, 6 tests`). 
- Ran the repository verification gate with `pnpm check`, which completed successfully (lint/type checks passed).

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

target.title: fix: avoid O(N²) shallow-copy in mapSensitivePaths schema traversal
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `mapSensitivePaths` performed `{ ...hints }` shallow copy on every recursive call while traversing the Zod schema tree, resulting in O(N²) allocation overhead.
- **Why it matters:** For the full `OpenClawSchema` tree (hundreds of nodes), this caused ~30s blocking on the main thread during config initialization.
- **What changed:** Extracted recursive logic into a private `mapSensitivePathsMut` helper that mutates `hints` in-place; the public `mapSensitivePaths` entry point now delegates to it.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Public API signature, return value semantics, logger initialization pattern, and all other functions in the file remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The original `mapSensitivePaths` spread the entire `hints` object (`{ ...hints }`) at every recursive node in the schema tree. With N schema nodes, this produced N shallow copies of a growing object — O(N²) total work.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No performance benchmark or test covering traversal time for the full `OpenClawSchema`.
- **Prior context:** This pattern was present since the original implementation of `mapSensitivePaths`.
- **Why this regressed now:** The schema tree has grown over time as more config fields and providers were added, making the quadratic cost increasingly visible.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/config/schema.hints.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** Existing tests already cover all structural Zod types (object, array, record, union, intersection, catchall) and validate the full `OpenClawSchema` mapping output.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The behavioral correctness is already fully tested; this was a pure performance issue, not a logic bug.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** `mapSensitivePaths` test suite in `src/config/schema.hints.test.ts` (lines 36–141).
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The change is a pure refactor preserving identical output. All 5 existing test cases validate the exact same assertions and pass without modification.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` (sensitive path detection logic is identical)
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Sequoia
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: Default

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/config/schema.hints.test.ts`
2. Observe all 5 test cases pass (including the full `OpenClawSchema` traversal test)

### Expected

- All tests pass; `mapSensitivePaths` returns identical hints as before.

### Actual

- All tests pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Before: O(N²) shallow copies — ~30s blocking for full OpenClawSchema tree.
After: O(N) single-pass in-place mutation — negligible traversal time.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Ran `pnpm test -- src/config/schema.hints.test.ts`; all 5 test cases pass. Confirmed output hints are identical to the previous implementation.
- **Edge cases checked:** All structural Zod types (object, array, record, union, discriminated union, intersection, catchall, optional/unwrappable wrappers) are covered by existing tests.
- **What you did NOT verify:** Real-world wall-clock timing benchmark on the full production config; relied on algorithmic complexity analysis (O(N²) → O(N)).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert this change quickly:** Revert the single commit; restore original `mapSensitivePaths` with per-call `{ ...hints }` spreading.
- **Files/config to restore:** `src/config/schema.hints.ts`
- **Known bad symptoms:** If the in-place mutation causes unexpected shared-state issues, callers would see stale or cross-contaminated `sensitive` flags in hints. Existing tests would catch this.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** `mapSensitivePaths` now mutates the passed-in `hints` object instead of returning a fresh copy. Callers that pass a shared hints object and expect immutability could see unexpected side effects.
  - **Mitigation:** Audited all call sites; callers either pass a freshly created `{}` or already consume the return value as the authoritative result. The public function signature and return semantics are unchanged.

target.title: Plugins: enforce private import boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins could couple directly to private OpenClaw internals or escape their package root through imports, even though the intended contract is the plugin SDK seam.
- Why it matters: That makes plugin behavior depend on unstable host internals and weakens the architecture boundary between plugin code and core.
- What changed: Added an enforced loader-time import policy that allows `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`, declared package dependencies, Node builtins, and in-package relative imports, while blocking private `openclaw/*` imports, undeclared bare packages, and imports that escape the plugin root.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not add process isolation or a sandbox; plugins still run arbitrary JS in-process.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Third-party and bundled plugins now fail to load when they import private OpenClaw package paths, undeclared bare packages, or files outside their own package root.
- Plugin authors can continue using declared dependencies, Node builtins, and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` seams.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Plugin code can no longer import private OpenClaw internals or escape its package root through the loader import path. This is contract hardening, not malicious-plugin sandboxing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader
- Relevant config (redacted): local temp plugin fixtures

### Steps

1. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/runtime` or `../outside.cjs`.
2. Load it through the plugin loader.
3. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`, a declared dependency, and an in-package helper.

### Expected

- Private OpenClaw imports and root-escaping imports are rejected before plugin execution.
- Declared dependencies, SDK seams, and in-package helpers continue to load.

### Actual

- Matches expected in direct local probes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm build`
  - repo `pnpm check` pipeline via `scripts/committer`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe rejecting `openclaw/runtime`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe allowing declared dependency + local helper import
- Edge cases checked:
  - plugin root escape via relative import
  - declared dependency allowlist from plugin-local `package.json`
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` suite completion in this worktree
  - real third-party plugins yet

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Mostly yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Maybe
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Plugin authors importing private OpenClaw internals must move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` or declare/package their own dependencies locally.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Revert commit `6a8e3526fa`
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/plugins/import-policy.ts`
  - `src/plugins/loader.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - plugins failing at load with `plugin import policy violation`
  - plugins relying on undeclared dependencies or private `openclaw/*` imports

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Existing plugins may rely on undeclared bare packages or private host imports and now fail fast.
  - Mitigation:
  - The failure is early and explicit, and the allowed path is narrow: declare the dependency or move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

target.title: Fix/hooks agent accountid normalization
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `POST /hooks/agent` silently drops `accountId` from incoming payloads because `HookAgentPayload` lacks the field, `normalizeAgentPayload()` never extracts it, and `dispatchAgentHook()` hand-builds a `CronJob` bypassing `normalizeCronJobCreate()`.
- Why it matters: Multi-account WhatsApp (and any channel requiring `accountId`) delivery fails silently — the caller gets a success response but the message is never sent.
- What changed: Hook dispatch now routes through `normalizeCronJobCreate()`, which promotes legacy flat fields into proper `CronDelivery` with `accountId`. `resolveCronToolPolicy()` derives `disableMessageTool` for shared-contract callers from whether the target is fully resolved (explicit mode + accountId) instead of unconditionally disabling.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The cron-owned path (`deliveryContract: "cron-owned"`) is untouched. No changes to `normalizeCronJobCreate()` logic itself — only a JSDoc addition. No new config options or API surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #43866
- Related #27015, #24186, #42244, #23853, #16925, #30052, #29090

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `POST /hooks/agent` now correctly preserves `accountId` through to delivery, enabling multi-account WhatsApp delivery via hooks.
- For shared-contract hook runs with a fully resolved delivery target (explicit mode + accountId), the agent's message tool is no longer unconditionally disabled — it remains available as a fallback. The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) prevents duplicate sends.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — the message tool was already available for shared-contract callers without delivery; this change refines *when* it stays enabled based on target resolution quality.
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: All (server-side code path)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any (not model/provider-specific)
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp (any multi-account channel)
- Relevant config (redacted): Hooks enabled with token, WhatsApp channel configured with multiple `accountId` connections

### Steps

1. Configure two WhatsApp connections with distinct `accountId` values.
2. Send `POST /hooks/agent` with `{ "message": "Hello", "channel": "whatsapp", "to": "1234567890", "accountId": "botnumber" }`.
3. Observe delivery result.

### Expected

- `accountId` flows through to `CronDelivery.accountId`, `resolveDeliveryTarget()` identifies the correct WhatsApp connection, and the message is delivered.

### Actual (before fix)

- `accountId` is silently dropped. Delivery fails because no connection can be identified. Caller receives success response.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

New tests added in second commit:
- `src/gateway/hooks.test.ts` — `normalizeAgentPayload` extracts and trims `accountId` (present, absent, blank)
- `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts` — integration test: `accountId` propagates into `job.delivery` via cron normalization pipeline
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts` — 3 tests for shared-contract tool policy: fully resolved target (explicit + accountId → enabled), missing accountId (→ disabled), implicitly resolved target (→ disabled)

Verification: `pnpm tsgo` clean, `pnpm check` clean, all 59 tests across the 4 test files pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: typecheck (`pnpm tsgo`), lint/format (`pnpm check`), unit + integration tests pass for all modified test files
- Edge cases checked: blank/missing `accountId`, `normalizeCronJobCreate` returning null (guard added), implicit vs explicit delivery target mode, `mode: "none"` (verified `deliveryRequested` is already `false` so no extra clause needed)
- What you did **not** verify: live end-to-end delivery through a real WhatsApp multi-account setup; only verified through unit/integration tests and code tracing

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert both commits; the hand-built CronJob path is fully self-contained in the reverted `dispatchAgentHook()`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/hooks.ts`, `src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, `src/cron/normalize.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Hook agent dispatches returning early with "normalizeCronJobCreate returned null" warning (would indicate the normalizer rejects a payload shape it previously accepted via hand-built path).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `normalizeCronJobCreate()` could reject a hook payload shape that the hand-built path previously accepted, causing hook dispatches to silently no-op.
  - Mitigation: A null-return guard logs a warning and still returns a `runId` to the caller. The normalizer is well-tested and accepts all fields the hook path provides. Integration test verifies the full pipeline.

- Risk: Enabling the message tool for shared-contract callers with fully resolved targets could cause duplicate sends (agent sends via tool + automatic delivery sends again).
  - Mitigation: The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) already detects when the agent's message tool sent to the same target and skips automatic delivery. This guard predates this change and is tested.

target.title: fix(gateway): surface cross-agent subagent sessions via allAgents flag (#95295)
target.body: Fixes #95295. ## What Problem This Solves Fixes an issue where users viewing the Control UI Sessions page or Recent Sessions sidebar for one agent could not see child sessions that were spawned from that workflow but stored under another configured agent. In the reported `dev` -> `orchestrator` case, the cross-agent subagent sessions existed in the session stores and could be listed from the CLI, but the web UI hid them unless they belonged to the currently selected agent. ## Why This Change Was Made This PR makes the UI use the existing `configuredAgentsOnly: true` plus omitted `agentId` request shape when the `All agents` toggle is enabled, instead of adding a new `sessions.list` protocol field. Explicit per-agent refreshes still pass `agentId`, so scoped loads stay narrow while the all-agents view can surface cross-agent child sessions in both the Sessions table and Recent Sessions sidebar. ## User Impact With `All agents` off, behavior is unchanged: the selected agent shows only its own sessions. With `All agents` on, operators can now see cross-agent child sessions in the Sessions table and Recent Sessions sidebar, including subagent runs spawned from another configured agent that were previously invisible. ## Evidence - Live behavior proof used a real `pnpm openclaw --dev gateway` setup with two configured agents (`dev`, `orchestrator`), not seeded store files. - Three real `sessions_spawn` calls from `agent:dev:main` created `agent:orchestrator:subagent:*` session records in the orchestrator store. The child runs later failed at model step in this dev environment because the OpenAI key returned `401`, but the session records were already persisted; this PR changes whether those persisted rows are visible in the UI. - Replace the three placeholders below with uploaded PR images after you attach them in GitHub: - `[IMAGE 1: Before fix - Recent Sessions sidebar only shows the current agent row]` - `[IMAGE 2: After fix - Recent Sessions sidebar shows current agent plus cross-agent orchestrator subagent rows with All agents enabled]` - `[IMAGE 3: Sessions page after fix - mixed dev and orchestrator rows visible in the same All agents view]` - Suggested captions once images are uploaded: - Before fix: cross-agent child sessions already exist in store, but the sidebar still shows only the current agent. - After fix: enabling `All agents` surfaces the cross-agent `agent:orchestrator:subagent:*` rows in Recent Sessions. - Sessions page after fix: the table now shows mixed `dev` and `orchestrator` rows in the same view. - Focused validation: - `pnpm test src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.sidebar.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts packages/gateway-protocol src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.subagent.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils` - `pnpm tsgo:core` - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` - `pnpm oxfmt --check` on touched files - `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` - `git diff --check`
